# Claims: Gamble, *The Palaeolithic Societies of Europe* (1999) — Range 2
**Source:** [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
**Full-book line range:** 3911–5490
**Slice file:** `range_2_3911_5490.txt` (1580 lines; slice-internal 1–1580)
**Coverage:** COMPLETE (slice starts mid-sentence continuing Ch.3 from prior range; ends mid-Ch.4 at garbled OCR of a site map figure after climatic-cycle discussion)
**Claim ID prefix:** R2-
**Actual coverage report:** Verified content = remainder of Ch.3 “A Palaeolithic framework: locales, rhythms and regions” (bridge from 1986 nine-region model; Table 3.1 locales/rhythms/regions; tacking; encounters/gatherings/social occasions/place; hearth ethnoarchaeology; Whitelaw/Binford camp patterning; attaching/detaching rituals; rhythms/practical consciousness; *chaîne opératoire*; paths/tracks; taskscape; landscape of habit vs social landscape; Ch.3 summary) + opening of Ch.4 “The first European societies 500,000–300,000 years ago” (defining Palaeolithic Europe; Pleistocene chronology/climates; cycle amplitude change). OCR noise: Figure 3.2 “Tacking” diagram text (lines ~100–140) partially garbled; end of slice (lines ~1551–1580) is unreadable map/site-name OCR — no claims extracted from that block.

---

### Claim R2-001: Bridge from 1986 Settlement book — ecology vs social relations
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]], [[social-landscape-palaeolithic]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) reports that in *The Palaeolithic Settlement of Europe* (1986a) he argued settlement behaviour was dominated by social over ecological relations (citing Ingold 1981), yet demonstrating that dominance was hard because the nine regions were large enough to encompass most social systems; Upper Palaeolithic case studies could show interregional contacts/exchanges, while periods before ~40,000 years ago seemed dominated by ecological rather than social relationships — a conclusion he now attributes retrospectively to a group-centred approach and ecological bridging arguments.
- **Quote:** "I also argued (Gamble 1986a:61) that as far as settlement behaviour was concerned social relations were dominant over ecological relations (Ingold 1981). … The periods prior to 40,000 years ago seemed dominated by ecological rather than social relationships … In retrospect this conclusion was unsurprising given that I took a group-centred approach … my bridging argument … was largely based on ecological priorities."
- **Notes:** Opens mid-paragraph; nine-region model and 775,000-year Middle Pleistocene climatic cycles mentioned in preceding sentences of the slice.

### Claim R2-002: Expanded framework — locales, rhythms, regions as narrative bridge
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[locale]], [[palaeolithic-rhythms]], [[social-landscape-palaeolithic]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) retains the regional scale and 1986 model (Figure 3.1) for later data chapters but expands the approach so locales and regions frame analysis of Palaeolithic societies with *rhythms* (the generators of social life) as the methodological bridge between theory and data, aiming for Gould’s (1994) “right level of abstraction.”
- **Quote:** "I have expanded the approach to use locales and regions as a framework for a detailed analysis of Palaeolithic societies but now with those rhythms, the generators of social life, acting as the bridge. … the three analytical levels — locales, rhythms and regions — have been identified."
- **Notes:** Explicit methodological redesign relative to 1986.

### Claim R2-003: Social archaeology from five million years ago; central question = release from proximity
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[release-from-proximity]], [[agency]], [[presence-and-co-presence]], [[palaeolithic]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) argues that focusing on individuals, network creation, and performance means social archaeology need not begin with the richer Upper Palaeolithic record but extends through the hominid record from five million years ago; the central question shifts from origins of band society (group closure, style, ethnicity) to explaining “release from proximity” (social relations stretched across time and space) and social complication.
- **Quote:** "social archaeology no longer begins with the richer record of the Upper Palaeolithic but instead extends throughout the hominid record, from five million years ago to the present day. Instead of the central question being the origins of band society … we are required to explain the ‘release from proximity’, where social relations came to be stretched across time and space, and the related question of social complication"
- **Notes:** Cites Gilman 1984, Williams 1974 on band society.

### Claim R2-004: Table 3.1 framework — LOCALES / RHYTHMS / REGIONS
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[locale]], [[palaeolithic-rhythms]], [[social-landscape-palaeolithic]], [[chaine-operatoire]], [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) presents Table 3.1 as the spatial-temporal framework for Palaeolithic society: LOCALES = Encounters, Gatherings, Social occasions, Place; RHYTHMS = Chaîne opératoire, *Taskscape, Paths & tracks; REGIONS = Landscape of habit, Social landscape; with analysis moving from INDIVIDUALS toward NETWORKS.
- **Quote:** "Table 3.1. A framework for studying Palaeolithic society. LOCALES … Encounters Gatherings Social occasions Place … RHYTHMS … Chaine opératoire * Taskscape Paths & tracks … REGIONS … Landscape of habit Social landscape … INDIVIDUALS > NETWORKS"
- **Notes:** Core taxonomy for later chapters; capture exact column structure for wiki concept pages.

### Claim R2-005: Method of “tacking” between scales of data and theory
- **Register:** METHOD / THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-clive]], [[processual-archaeology]], [[post-processual-archaeology]], [[palaeolithic]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999), following Wylie (1993:23–4), treats Palaeolithic interpretation as “tacking” (yacht metaphor, Figure 3.2) between theory and data of different resolutions — from mint-fresh “flagship sites” to coarse palimpsests of rolled tools in river terraces — contrasting scales from ~15-minute episodes half a million years ago to assemblages averaged over 70,000-year chunks; tacking works because structure of the record reveals attainable temporal resolution.
- **Quote:** "analysis can begin by treating the Palaeolithic as an interpretative exercise where we ‘tack’, rather like a yacht (Figure 3.2), between data and theory (Wylie 1993:23-4). … we can recover fifteen-minute episodes that took place half a million years ago and we also recover assemblages averaged across 70,000-year chunks of time."
- **Notes:** Also cites Gamble 1996b; patches vs scatters; ecological vs geological time in Figure 3.2 caption.

### Claim R2-006: Locales (not sites) as where interaction is preserved
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[locale]], [[agency]], [[presence-and-co-presence]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) defines locales, rather than sites, as where interaction took place and is preserved; analysis starts from the individual moving through the environment with ambulatory perception (Gibson 1979) along paths and tracks.
- **Quote:** "Locales, rather than sites, are where interaction took place and is preserved. … The starting point is the individual and her/his movement through their environment. This involves ambulatory perception (Gibson 1979) along paths and tracks."
- **Notes:** Terminological shift from settlement-site vocabulary.

### Claim R2-007: Encounter as path-intersection node; forager/collector patterns
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[locale]], [[binford-lewis]], [[ethnoarchaeology]], [[ingold-tim]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) models an encounter as the locale/node where paths intersect and perceptual interaction occurs (animal, person, plants, raw materials); both Binford’s (1980) encounter-pattern foragers and intercept-pattern collectors can be described via ambulatory perception and affordances, illustrated by a hunter’s trapline (Figure 3.3; Ingold 1986; Brody 1981 Fort Nelson/Prophet River paths).
- **Quote:** "Where their path intersects with another we can describe a locale or node where such perceptual interaction takes place. … Both the encounter pattern of foragers and the intercept pattern of collectors (Binford 1980) can be described in these terms."
- **Notes:** Affordances = use values along the track.

### Claim R2-008: Encounters leave no archaeological residue
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[locale]], [[palaeolithic]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) holds that encounters are information “sound-bites” about the environmental track and do not result in a residue that will survive archaeologically.
- **Quote:** "Encounters are information ‘sound-bites’ about the environmental track along which the hominid is travelling. They do not result in a residue that will survive."
- **Notes:** Distinguishes encounters from gatherings.

### Claim R2-009: Gatherings as enduring locales with residues
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[locale]], [[presence-and-co-presence]], [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[release-from-proximity]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) defines gatherings as enduring locales because of quality/quantity of residues from single or multiple visits; gatherings are the encounters that have survived. Information may be embodied (words, gestures requiring bodily presence) or disembodied (art, tracks, artifacts) — the latter extends co-presence and intimate/effective networks *in absentia*.
- **Quote:** "Gatherings are enduring locales. They endure because of the quality and quantities of the residues left behind. … Gatherings are the encounters which have survived. … such intensity which extends intimate and effective networks in absentia … also structures the archaeological record."
- **Notes:** Adapts Goffman (1963); residue is the sole archaeological criterion for a gathering, not necessarily multi-person interaction.

### Claim R2-010: Hearth as robust spatial signature of gathering
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[locale]], [[ethnoarchaeology]], [[palaeolithic]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) argues gatherings use portable resources (food, stone, bodies) that structure interaction and the archaeological record; the hearth preserves the gathering in residue patterning robust enough to survive a range of taphonomic processes (Stapert 1992, Whallon 1984).
- **Quote:** "the hearth preserves the gathering in the patterning of the residues which build up around it as interaction takes place. These patterns remain robust enough to survive a range of taphonomic processes (Stapert 1992, Whallon 1984)."
- **Notes:** Materials as extension of social performance (cf. Leroi-Gourhan gesture later).

### Claim R2-011: Conversational group-size limits (~4 people; ~1.7 m)
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[locale]], [[presence-and-co-presence]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) reports Dunbar’s (1993:690–1) evidence that human conversational group sizes are limited to about four people (one speaker + three listeners); cliques form in multiples of four; speech attenuation and ~1.7 m nose-to-nose upper limit for comfortable dyadic conversation; ~0.5 m shoulder spacing allows a five-person ring before a new clique forms (Figure 3.4).
- **Quote:** "Dunbar (1993:690-1) has presented evidence which shows that human conservational group sizes are limited to about four people (one speaker plus three listeners). … Dunbar (1993) cites evidence that 1.7m, nose to nose, forms the upper limit for comfortable conversation between dyads."
- **Notes:** OCR: “conservational” almost certainly “conversational.” Attributed findings via Dunbar.

### Claim R2-012: Mask Site ethnoarchaeology — hearth seating dimensions
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[binford-lewis]], [[ethnoarchaeology]], [[locale]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) summarizes Binford’s (1978a/1983) Mask Site (Anaktuvak Pass, Alaska) Nunamiut hunting-stand study: around hearths dominant activities were eating and conversation; conversation groups rarely more than four or five; Table 3.2 seating metrics (e.g. 3–4 men: mean kneecap-to-ember 62±6.8 cm, knee spacing 33±4 cm; 5 men: tighter spacing); hearths 50–75 cm diameter; opposite-side distances ~174–217 cm depending on group size — comparable to Dunbar’s standing figures.
- **Quote:** "An ethnoarchaeological example of such a conversation ring is provided by Binford’s (1978a) study of a Nunamiut hunting stand. … Conversation groups were rarely more than four or five people. … men seated on opposite sides of the fire would be 190 to 217 cm apart when five were in the group and 174 to 199cm when four were seated."
- **Notes:** Table 3.2 numbers partially OCR-garbled for the five-person row.

### Claim R2-013: Mask Site drop zone and toss zone
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[binford-lewis]], [[ethnoarchaeology]], [[locale]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) reports Binford’s size-sorted discard: small items in a drop zone ring ~50–100 cm from the fire edge; larger items tossed through the fire or over the shoulder into a toss zone ~2.25–2.75 m from that edge (Figure 3.4).
- **Quote:** "The drop zone forms a ring some 50 to 100cm from the edge of the fire. The toss zone lies some 2.25 to 2.75m from that same edge (Figure 3.4)."
- **Notes:** Classic middle-range spatial model for Palaeolithic hearths.

### Claim R2-014: Critique of body-size-only spatial interpretation; content not implicit in pattern
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[binford-lewis]], [[locale]], [[ethnoarchaeology]], [[post-processual-archaeology]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999), following Whitelaw (1994), argues that if body size rather than performances is the means to interpret spatial data from caves and open locales (Binford 1983a), studies remain mechanical/descriptive; body faculties set communication conditions but interaction content is not implicit in the spatial patterns produced; without material culture there is no archaeologically visible symbolic partitioning of space.
- **Quote:** "so long as the size of the body, rather than the performances in which it was involved, is the means to interpret our spatial information from caves and open locales (Binford 1983a) then, as Whitelaw (1994) has argued, such studies remain mechanical and descriptive. … the content of the interactions which such acts service is not implicit in the spatial patterns they produce."
- **Notes:** Cites Figure 3.5 !Kung hearth-side model after Whitelaw 1994.

### Claim R2-015: Mask Site function was interaction, not primarily game-watching
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[binford-lewis]], [[ethnoarchaeology]], [[locale]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) reports Binford’s conclusion that the Mask Site’s function was not primarily to watch for game but to engage in boredom-reducing pursuits (e.g. cards); lonelier pursuits (mask making, game watching) occurred when gatherings were small; gathering size and hearths act as uniformitarian anchors; interaction pattern changes with gathering size even if social context may not.
- **Quote:** "The function of the Mask Site, it emerges, was not to watch for game as we might suppose, but rather to engage in pursuits which reduced boredom, such as playing cards … Gathering size and material structures such as hearths both act as uniformitarian anchors to the past."
- **Notes:** Attributed to Binford 1983b[1978].

### Claim R2-016: Materials at gatherings as gestures / extensions of the actor
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[locale]], [[chaine-operatoire]], [[agency]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) treats materials associated with gatherings as extensions of the social actor — a gesture (Leroi-Gourhan 1993; White 1993a) synonymous with material action; portable resources’ affordance in social transactions is linked to association with the body in performance; competent members may bring only the body, skilled practitioners carry externalized resources.
- **Quote:** "Materials associated with gatherings are therefore best regarded as an extension of the social actor, a gesture as described by Leroi-Gourhan (1993), where gesture is synonymous with material action (White 1993a)."
- **Notes:** Links Table 2.8 resources to locale performance.

### Claim R2-017: Social occasions vs gatherings; place without architecture
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[locale]], [[ingold-tim]], [[social-landscape-palaeolithic]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) distinguishes social occasions from gatherings: social occasions involve performance context established by objects disembodied from the actor, commonly architecture and non-portable objects, but a built environment is not necessary — named locales invested with associations/meanings are termed *place*, citing Ingold’s (1993:155) definition of place via experiences and ambience afforded by activities.
- **Quote:** "I draw a further distinction between gatherings and social occasions … The latter involve a context for performance which is established by objects disembodied from the actor. … a built environment is not necessary for a social occasion to happen. It can occur at a named locale invested with associations and meanings. In order to avoid confusion I refer to such social occasions as place"
- **Notes:** Table 3.1 column LOCALES includes both Social occasions and Place.

### Claim R2-018: Caves as places that structure performance (San comparative example)
- **Register:** FINDING / THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[locale]], [[ethnoarchaeology]], [[palaeolithic]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) treats caves as classic Palaeolithic *places*: natural affordances (shelter; also hyena, owl, bear residues) whose size/aspect/head height/floor surface structure behaviour without being symbolically created architecture; citing Parkington and Mills (1991) on San open windbreak camps vs caves — caves allow privacy without constant communal monitoring but preclude close arm’s-length relationships of windbreak camps.
- **Quote:** "That classic Palaeolithic site type, the cave, provides an example of place. … As Parkington and Mills (1991) show … the use of the latter must have been fundamentally different because of the arena they defined for social performance. The caves allowed privacy where no constant communal monitoring of herding or sharing was possible."
- **Notes:** Also Les Eyzies/Vézère as multi-millennial place of social occasions.

### Claim R2-019: Attention and ontological security at locales
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[locale]], [[presence-and-co-presence]], [[agency]], [[giddens-anthony]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999), following Wilson (1988:4), argues visual attention at gatherings is prime for humans and non-human primates because it contributes to ontological security (natural and social worlds as they appear); locales are where social life is performed and attention occurs; what matters is resources actors bring (bodies, objects) and their replication in time/space — not structures that precede individuals or society preceding actors.
- **Quote:** "Locales are above all where social life is performed, where attention occurs. Moreover we do not have to specify, these micro-scale contexts, such as gatherings, through structures which precede the individual. Neither, at a macro scale, do we need to hold to a notion that society precedes its actors."
- **Notes:** Networks as pretext/context for co-presence (Goffman 1963:247 quoted).

### Claim R2-020: Whitelaw global hunter-gatherer sample — density falls with camp population
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[ethnoarchaeology]], [[locale]], [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) reports Whitelaw’s (1991, 1989, 1994) analysis of 1,762 social occasions in 800 communities and 124 hunter-gatherer cultures: occupation density (persons/hectare) falls dramatically as camp population increases; variation in camp layout (shelter distances; circular vs linear) related to social relations of production, especially co-operation and sharing as risk-coping (Figure 3.6).
- **Quote:** "The data come from 1,762 different social occasions in 800 communities and 124 different hunter-gatherer cultures (Whitelaw 1991:141). … occupation density (the number of people per hectare) falls dramatically as the population size of the camp increases."
- **Notes:** Three habitat solutions in Figure 3.7.

### Claim R2-021: Habitat patterning of camp density (Arctic/arid vs rainforest vs grassland)
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[ethnoarchaeology]], [[locale]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) summarizes Whitelaw’s three global solutions: (1) Arctic and Australian arid zones — self-sufficient households, large low-density camps; (2) tropical rainforests — high co-operation, small high-density camps (e.g. Efe Pygmy, Zaire); (3) savannahs/grasslands — widest range from low- to high-density, reflecting mosaic habitats and variable sharing/co-operation.
- **Quote:** "Firstly, in the Arctic and Australian arid zones where households are generally self-sufficient, campsites cover large areas with households widely spaced. Secondly, in the tropical rainforests, co-operation is often the best strategy … Hence the small, high-density Efe Pygmy camps … Finally, it is in the savannahs and grasslands that the greatest range of camping arrangements is found."
- **Notes:** High density generally indicates co-operation; low density household independence (Figure 3.7 caption).

### Claim R2-022: Binford Nunamiut — work vs consumption, not kinship, structures spacing
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[binford-lewis]], [[ethnoarchaeology]], [[locale]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) reports Binford’s (1991) Nunamiut camp analysis: camping patterns structured by organization of workforce and consumers rather than kinship; summer (lowest security, lean caribou) yields flexible multi-party hunting from large camps but *greater* tent spacing (mean 69.7 m) than winter/fall (26.2–29.9 m) when living on stored food and household independence is greatest — co-operation in procurement without close residential clustering, contrasting Whitelaw’s high-density=co-operation model while agreeing that social structure (variable interaction levels) is preserved in spatial patterning.
- **Quote:** "He concluded that their camping patterns were structured by the organization of the workforce and consumers (ibid.:128) rather than by kinship. … on summer sites the campers are further apart (mean distance between tents 69.7m) than at any other time of the year … In the winter and fall all campers are closer together (mean distance between tents 26.2-29.9 m)"
- **Notes:** Figure 3.8 comparative mobile HG settlements.

### Claim R2-023: Bilzingsleben gathering vs Dolní Věstonice social occasion
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[bilzingsleben]], [[dolni-vestonice]], [[locale]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) contrasts archaeologically different locales for “unfreezing” social performance: Lower Palaeolithic Bilzingsleben as a gathering (lake, trees, stone and bone anvils; Mania 1990; Ch.4) versus Upper Palaeolithic Dolní Věstonice as a social occasion based on a built environment of huts, graves, ovens and middens (Svoboda 1991; Ch.7).
- **Quote:** "the Lower Palaeolithic gathering of Bilzingsleben with its lake, trees, stone and bone anvils (Mania 1990) (Chapter 4) and Upper Palaeolithic Dolni Véstonice, where a social occasion was based on a built environment of huts, graves, ovens and middens (Svoboda 1991) (Chapter 7)."
- **Notes:** Illustrative pair for gathering vs social occasion typology.

### Claim R2-024: Attaching and detaching rituals; elephant analogy to intimate/effective networks
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[locale]], [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[presence-and-co-presence]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999), drawing on Goffman (1959, 1963), treats attaching/detaching rituals as structured engagement/disengagement from gatherings and social occasions; non-linguistic greeting ceremonies in chimpanzees and elephants show intensity of such rituals among highly social mammals; elephant family unit (~8) and bond group (~20) said to bear similarity to human intimate and effective networks because of similar relationship-defining resources (Table 2.8).
- **Quote:** "Attaching and detaching rituals represent intense interactions for any highly social mammal … It is by such attaching, or greeting, rituals that an elephant defines its society as a network of negotiated relationships (Moss 1988:125). The elephant family unit of eight animals, and bond group of twenty, bears some similarity to our intimate and effective networks"
- **Notes:** Analogy, not identity claim; attributed to Moss 1988, Reynolds 1966.

### Claim R2-025: Rhythms create space, time, and forms (Leroi-Gourhan)
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[palaeolithic-rhythms]], [[chaine-operatoire]], [[agency]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) defines body rhythms (walking, treading, digesting, sleeping, making) as producing temporality uniting social and technical behaviour; quotes Leroi-Gourhan (1993:309): rhythms create space and time for the individual and create forms when materialized in a rhythmic frame.
- **Quote:** "Rhythms are the creators of space and time, at least for the individual. Space and time do not enter lived experience until they are materialised within a rhythmic frame. Rhythms are also the creators of forms. (Leroi-Gourhan 1993:309, my emphasis)"
- **Notes:** Section “Rhythms and social technology.”

### Claim R2-026: Practical vs discursive consciousness (Giddens) and habitual action
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[giddens-anthony]], [[agency]], [[palaeolithic-rhythms]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) grounds social structure in individual agency via Table 3.3 (after Giddens 1984:7): discursive consciousness (what actors can say about conditions of action), practical consciousness (what they know/believe but cannot express discursively — habit/routine), and unconscious motives; many rhythms are habitual (Gosden 1994); Leroi-Gourhan’s “mechanical operational sequences” in a “psychological twilight” form the basis of individual survival behaviour.
- **Quote:** "Elsewhere Giddens (1984) has described this as practical consciousness and has drawn the distinction between this and a discursive or problem-solving consciousness (Table 3.3). … ‘mechanical operational sequences [practical consciousness] form the basis of individual behaviour; they are our essential element of survival’ (1993:232)."
- **Notes:** Cartesian dualism critique; Heidegger/Husserl/Dreyfus gear-shift example; Marlowe vs Holmes detective metaphor for being-in-the-world vs detached deduction.

### Claim R2-027: Chaîne opératoire broader than lithic reduction sequence
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[chaine-operatoire]], [[palaeolithic-rhythms]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) argues the *chaîne opératoire* (operational sequence) fuses social and technical activity (citing Boëda, Geneste, Meignen, Schlanger, White, etc.) but has unfortunately become synonymous with lithic reduction; Schlanger’s (1994:144) “interplay between fixed and flexible” better captures the spirit — physical laws fix material action in part, social/cultural contexts supply flexible choices; technical acts are simultaneously social acts; style/function dichotomy dissolves when both are one social domain (White 1993a).
- **Quote:** "Unfortunately, the chaine opératoire has become synonymous with ‘lithic reduction sequence’ … I believe Schlanger is closer to the spirit of the concept when he describes the chaine opératoire as examining ‘the interplay between fixed and flexible’ (1994:144). … Leroi-Gourhan’s essential point was therefore that technical acts are at the same time social acts (Schlanger 1990:23)."
- **Notes:** Karlin and Julien 1994:164 definition of ordered train of actions also quoted.

### Claim R2-028: Critique of Leroi-Gourhan’s collective/symbolic exteriorization
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[chaine-operatoire]], [[gamble-clive]], [[release-from-proximity]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999), citing Graves (1994:440), argues Leroi-Gourhan did not fully integrate the social: he insisted on a collective view of society and Cartesian separation of internal/external environments via symbolic projection — “maintaining a distance between lived experience and the organism” (1993:234–5) — so individuals disappear into the collective with each exteriorizing technical advance; progressive shift from natural/corporeal to artificial/symbolic rhythms is implied without explanation of why changes occurred — helping explain why *chaîne opératoire* has been used more for lithics than social action.
- **Quote:** "Graves (1994:440) has pointed out that Leroi-Gourhan did not, however, fully integrate the social. He insisted on a collective view of society … As a result, the individual gradually disappears into the collective world with each technical advance … it is not difficult to see why the chaine opératoire has been used to analyse lithic reduction rather than social action."
- **Notes:** Gamble’s partial uptake of Leroi-Gourhan.

### Claim R2-029: Artifacts in gatherings — reject function/style split; narrow symbolism
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[chaine-operatoire]], [[agency]], [[release-from-proximity]], [[presence-and-co-presence]], [[ingold-tim]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) argues objects in gatherings, if performances rest on concepts of relationships, are charged as symbolic extensions of the body-in-performance; technology is active in stasis/change of social life, not passive “style” after function (contra Sackett 1982 style-as-identity); yet he reserves a *narrow* definition of symbolic behaviour for interaction between people away from co-presence of gatherings — when artifacts become personified (Ingold 1994:335) and release from proximity is achieved; Leroi-Gourhan’s tool “really exists” when animated by gestures (Schlanger 1990:20).
- **Quote:** "I would therefore argue for a narrow definition of symbolic behaviour. I would reserve it for interaction between people which takes place away from the co-presence found in gatherings. … When artifacts become people we have achieved that release from proximity which is our primate social heritage."
- **Notes:** Key theoretical boundary for symbolism.

### Claim R2-030: Paths and tracks over catchment territories
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[palaeolithic-rhythms]], [[locale]], [[social-landscape-palaeolithic]], [[ethnoarchaeology]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) holds that *chaîne opératoire* also links locales via paths and tracks; for foragers, intersection points (prey, plants) matter more than surface-area territories — supported by boreal traplines (Brody, Nelson), Mbuti paths synonymous with home range/territory (Bahuchet 1992), Nukak tracks (Politis 1996), and Australian estates as places on tracks (Stanner 1965); world-image as itinerary (Leroi-Gourhan 1993:326) not concentric site catchment (Vita-Finzi and Higgs 1970).
- **Quote:** "The locales and paths, rather than the surface-area territories which surround them, are the important elements in the foragers’ socially constructed landscapes. … The image that foragers have of the world is linked to an itinerary (Leroi-Gourhan 1993:326), not to a concentric surface area as proposed by site catchment analysis (Vita-Finzi and Higgs 1970)."
- **Notes:** Ambulatory perception (Gibson 1979) again.

### Claim R2-031: Taskscape — continuous activities, sound, social temporality (Ingold)
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[ingold-tim]], [[palaeolithic-rhythms]], [[chaine-operatoire]], [[locale]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) adopts Ingold’s (1993) taskscape as array of related activities carrying forward social life (landscape = array of related features); continuous character; activity produces sound — landscape is what we see, taskscape what we hear; skills (walking, listening, making) learned in social life; temporality of taskscape is social because people attend to one another in performance (Ingold 1993:159–60); close to *chaîne opératoire* but re-engages individuals in the social world (Gosden 1994).
- **Quote:** "The taskscape is an array of related activities just as, in his terms, a landscape is an array of related features (ibid.:158). … The landscape is what we see and the taskscape is what we hear (ibid.:163). … The temporality of the taskscape is social … because people, in the performance of their tasks, also attend to one another."
- **Notes:** OCR “arfay” = array.

### Claim R2-032: Landscape of habit renamed from local hominid network
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[social-landscape-palaeolithic]], [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[giddens-anthony]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) renames his earlier “local hominid network” (Gamble 1993d, 1995b, 1996a, 1998) the *landscape of habit* (after Gosden 1994:182) to avoid confusion with personal-network terminology: a common structure in forager space-time behaviour encompassing subsistence and social behaviour, centred on the individual; everyday routines at locales structure interaction contexts; routinization as Giddens’s (1984:376) habitual, taken-for-granted bulk of day-to-day social life.
- **Quote:** "to avoid confusion with other uses in this book of the term network, I have renamed this concept the landscape of habit, as described by Gosden (1994:182). This change of term emphasizes how the everyday routines which are performed at locales also have a temporal sequence that structures the contexts of interaction."
- **Notes:** Regions half of Table 3.1.

### Claim R2-033: Raw-material principle and scale of landscape of habit (Geneste)
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[social-landscape-palaeolithic]], [[mousterian-industry]], [[chaine-operatoire]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) presents Geneste’s (1988a, 1988b) Middle Palaeolithic SW France raw-material data (Table 3.4) and a general forager principle: distant raw materials are represented by later stages of the reduction sequence; “distant” = ~30–80 km transfers (local ≤5 km: 55–98% of stone, 1–5% utilized as tools; regional 5–20 km: 2–20% of stone; distant 30–80 km: <5% of stone but 74–100% utilized); worldwide sample yields common landscape-of-habit scale ~40 km radius, upper limit ~100 km; within this radius activities are “local” socially/organizationally.
- **Quote:** "distant raw materials are represented by the later stages of the reduction sequence (Gamble 1993d:36). … From a larger worldwide sample the raw-material data indicate a common scale for the landscape of habit with a radius of 40km and an upper limit of 100km (Gamble 1993d:42)."
- **Notes:** Same relationships said to hold for Upper Palaeolithic and many regions; operational sequence embedded in mobility network.

### Claim R2-034: Aka exploration ranges as ethnographic support for landscape-of-habit scale
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[ethnoarchaeology]], [[social-landscape-palaeolithic]], [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) cites Hewlett et al. (1986) on Aka visits: 1–175 km, 96% <100 km, half <50 km; reasons and mean distances (Table 3.5) — family visits 47.6 km, hunting 22.8 km, dancing/ceremonies 17.6 km, work for villagers 66.4 km; exploration range (median half-range) men 58 km, women 32 km, combined average 35 km; density affects post-marital distance (high-density Bagandou vs low-density Ndele).
- **Quote:** "visits among the Aka ranged from 1 to 175km, with 96 per cent being less than 100 km and half of all visits less than 50km (Table 3.5). … Men had a larger exploration range of 58 km compared to 32 km for women. The combined average was 35km."
- **Notes:** Tropical rainforest setting caveat acknowledged.

### Claim R2-035: Wobst mating networks — ~300 km threshold
- **Register:** FINDING
- **Target pages:** [[social-landscape-palaeolithic]], [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) reviews Wobst’s (1976) modelled forager mating systems of 175–475 people (Table 3.6): spatial extent varies with density; maximum ~300 km diameter suggested as threshold beyond which costs of maintaining a closed system (ritual, exchange, food sharing) become prohibitive; individuals’ exploration ranges and marriage moves unlikely to exceed 300 km and mostly under ~80 km — scales of intimate/effective (and possibly extended) networks; antiquity supported by Steele home-range estimates 32–102 km diameter (Table 2.10).
- **Quote:** "Wobst’s figures suggest a maximum of some 300km. He predicts that this figure marks a threshold for mating-network size beyond which the costs of interaction to maintain a closed system … would become prohibitive. … are unlikely to exceed 300km, and while necessarily variable, are probably mostly under 80 km"
- **Notes:** Landscape of habit as common hominid heritage.

### Claim R2-036: Social landscape defined — extended/global networks; tracks, travel, trespass, trade
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[social-landscape-palaeolithic]], [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[release-from-proximity]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) defines the *social landscape* by the scale negotiations achieve: spatial outcome of individuals developing extended networks and appearance of the global network (Tables 2.8–2.9); Australian Aboriginal networks of “tracks, travel, trespass and trade” (Tindale 1974:75); redefines personal-network resource use; produces surface structure of the *group* as summation of intersecting individual networks with a “life” beyond the individual; group defined by the “other” through the global network.
- **Quote:** "The social landscape is distinguished as a spatial concept by the scale which negotiations achieve. It is the spatial outcome of individuals developing their extended network and the appearance of the global network … In Tindale’s apt phrase for aboriginal Australia, these networks deal with ‘tracks, travel, trespass and trade’ (1974:75)."
- **Notes:** Encompasses without replacing landscapes of habit; bottom-up groups as unintended consequences.

### Claim R2-037: Table 3.7 — mapping social/primate/Palaeolithic network schemes
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[social-landscape-palaeolithic]], [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[locale]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) Table 3.7 aligns Boissevain (1968) social networks, Hinde (1976) primate networks, and his Palaeolithic conceptual structure: Individuals/Interaction → Locales; intimate/effective (and Relationships) → Landscape of habit; extended/global (Groups, Institutional complexes, Society) → Social landscapes at the regional scale — clarifying relative positions without strict equivalence.
- **Quote:** "Table 3.7. Models of human and primate societies compared with a structure for the evolution of Palaeolithic society. … Interactional networks … Locales … intimate … Landscape of habit … effective … extended … global … Social landscapes"
- **Notes:** Landscape of habit = full personal network = Hinde social structure = regional concept.

### Claim R2-038: Extended network as key Palaeolithic change; society without social landscape still society
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[social-landscape-palaeolithic]], [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[release-from-proximity]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) contends Palaeolithic society is not only present when a social landscape can be claimed — without a social landscape there is a *different form* of society; landscape of habit already has intimate, effective, and extended ties; what changed was character and extent of the regional scale; the extended network was key because it enabled negotiation of global networks and restructuring of resources/culture under a social landscape rather than landscape of habit.
- **Quote:** "the scheme is not meant to imply that Palaeolithic society only existed when a social landscape can be claimed. Rather it is the case that without a social landscape we have a different form of society. … It is my contention that the extended network was the key to this change because it led to the possibility of individuals negotiating their global networks."
- **Notes:** Central thesis linking Ch.2 networks to regional archaeology.

### Claim R2-039: Social landscape = distanciation, release from proximity, complex→complicated
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[release-from-proximity]], [[social-landscape-palaeolithic]], [[presence-and-co-presence]], [[giddens-anthony]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) argues the social landscape achieves distanciation/stretching of social systems across space-time and release from proximity; negotiations fixed by *possibility* of interaction whether or not it occurs — societies of distant relatives and comparative strangers; shift from complex to complicated social life (Strum and Latour 1987; Rowell 1991) via symbols and material resources simplifying tasks so individuals handle greater social elaboration; inclusive as well as exclusive systems; objects contribute to power at a distance (Gosden 1994); symbolic/material bonds make actors appear to enter pre-existing structure (Strum and Latour 1987:796).
- **Quote:** "The social landscape therefore achieves the distanciation, or stretching, of social systems across space-time. It is through the social landscape that the actions of individuals achieve that release from proximity … Most importantly the social landscape sees a shift from social life being complex to becoming complicated"
- **Notes:** Landscape of habit characterized as local and exclusive.

### Claim R2-040: Objects become people through exchange in the social landscape
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[social-landscape-palaeolithic]], [[chaine-operatoire]], [[release-from-proximity]], [[giddens-anthony]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) contrasts landscapes of habit — objects move *with* people as portable affordances embedded in hunting, visiting, attaching/detaching rituals (McBryde: exchange embedded in social/ceremonial life) — with social landscapes where objects become people: transfers express wider social structures; exchange gestures comparable to manufacturing gestures as operational sequences; material action incorporates group-level social representations; produces Giddensian time-space distanciation and system integration *in absentia*.
- **Quote:** "The difference with the social landscape is that in a very real sense objects now become people. As artifacts and manuports are transferred between individuals they express relationships which refer to wider social structures. … Social systems are stretched due to the mechanisms of system integration, the means by which they are extended in absentia"
- **Notes:** McBryde Central Australia exchange quote.

### Claim R2-041: Against culture-explains-culture / symbolic-domestication teleology
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[social-landscape-palaeolithic]], [[agency]], [[gamble-clive]], [[release-from-proximity]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) rejects explanations that treat emergence of symbolism/language as the final humanizing ingredient (Leroi-Gourhan’s “symbolic domestication” from seasonal to symbol-packaged rhythmicity; White 1959 culture-as-adaptation; individual submerged in group) as circular “culture explaining culture”; answers with social landscape as sum of individual networks — active agents never surrender to collective tyranny of culture; social systems inherited are also changeable; people become named “places” in networks (personified places/paths).
- **Quote:** "The problem with this familiar explanation … is that it starts to look suspiciously like culture explaining culture. This is no explanation at all. My answer to such a circular argument is the social landscape, which is the sum total of individual networks. … they never surrender to this collectivity of symbols, this tyranny of culture."
- **Notes:** Agency-centred alternative to progressive exteriorization narratives.

### Claim R2-042: Scale of social landscape — ethnographic long-distance transfers; no magic prestige distance
- **Register:** FINDING / THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[social-landscape-palaeolithic]], [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[ethnoarchaeology]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) states no upper spatial limit to the social landscape (potential mobility); taskscape limited by auditory world (Ingold); landscape of habit upper limit 80–100 km; ethnographic transfers of stone etc. (Féblot-Augustins and Perlés 1992) document 600–3,600 km among HGs; McBryde Australian axe trade — Mt William greenstone moved up to 800 km, average 228 km — domain of social value/affiliation not mere utility; prestige/exotic transformation (Gould 1980; Helms 1988) has no magic distance (could be 5 or 500 km) — depends on social contexts of network negotiation; inclusive systems enable tolerated trespass (Hill 1978; Peterson 1986 inclusive/exclusive poles).
- **Quote:** "Transfer of materials over 600 to 3,600km among hunters and gatherers can be readily documented. … stone from the greenstone quarries of Victoria was being moved as much as 800km and on average 228km from the Mt William source. … There is no magic distance beyond which an axe always ceases to be an axe and becomes a prestige good … It could be 5km or 500km."
- **Notes:** Mode of transfer less important than demonstrating extended social landscape tracks longer than any individual’s ambulatory perception.

### Claim R2-043: Ch.3 summary — new vocabulary for society not settlement
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[locale]], [[palaeolithic-rhythms]], [[social-landscape-palaeolithic]], [[binford-lewis]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) summarizes: locales and regions linked by rhythms (paths/tracks, operational sequences, taskscapes); archaeological locale terms are encounter, gathering, social occasion/place rather than campsites, home bases, satellite camps, or Binford’s (1980) residential camp/location/caches/field stations — those terms remain useful for settlement analysis but are theory-laden by dichotomous forager approaches (Table 1.3); fresh vocabulary declares agenda shift from Palaeolithic settlement to Palaeolithic society.
- **Quote:** "I have used these terms rather than the more familiar campsites, home bases, satellite camps, or Binford’s (1980) distinctions between residential camp, location, caches and field stations. … The point about a fresh vocabulary is its declaration that a new agenda is being explored: in this case a study of Palaeolithic society rather than Palaeolithic settlement."
- **Notes:** Explicit bridge from 1986 settlement book.

### Claim R2-044: Archaeological culture as repeated individual gestures, not collective mind
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[chaine-operatoire]], [[agency]], [[palaeolithic]], [[gamble-clive]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) argues social archaeology involves all aspects of mobility, production, consumption and discard because technical acts are social; repetition and persistence of material action produce the archaeological culture as results of individuals repeating learned technical gestures rather than a collective group mind producing a pattern to be followed.
- **Quote:** "Repetition and persistence of material action in time and space produce the well-known entity of the archaeological culture. These entities are the results of individuals repeating the technical gestures they learned rather than the collective mind of a group producing a pattern of culture to be followed."
- **Notes:** Bottom-up reading of culture-historical entities.

### Claim R2-045: Landscape of habit vs social landscape — exclusion only vs inclusive/exclusive; strangers
- **Register:** THEORY
- **Target pages:** [[social-landscape-palaeolithic]], [[intimate-effective-extended-networks]], [[release-from-proximity]], [[presence-and-co-presence]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) proposes: landscape of habit = Hinde-style social structure with weak extended networks, nonexistent global network, exclusion alone, dimensions from raw-material distributions; social landscape = historical development with material resources as social objects/symbols, exclusive *and* inclusive systems, group as property of network density/scale/intensity, strangers relative not absolute (vs absolute strangers possible in landscape of habit), co-presence limits removed so society can grow almost without geographical limit.
- **Quote:** "Within this structure individuals build their own networks but their extended networks are only weakly developed and the global network is nonexistent. The social principle is one of exclusion alone. … The social landscape could therefore contain both exclusive and inclusive social systems. … Strangers were never absolute in the social landscape but instead formed a relative social category … In contrast to the landscape of habit where absolute strangers could potentially exist."
- **Notes:** Closing theoretical contrast of Ch.3.

### Claim R2-046: Not a single-factor transition; Europe = long-term innovation + population replacement
- **Register:** POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[social-landscape-palaeolithic]], [[neanderthals]], [[gamble-clive]], [[palaeolithic]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) denies that landscape-of-habit → social-landscape development reduces to a single factor (enhanced memory, language, discursive consciousness, or technology) or a gradual progressive exteriorization of memory through symbols; in Palaeolithic Europe it was both a process of long-term innovation and contingent upon the replacement of population — concepts directed at stasis and change to be tested against data narratives.
- **Quote:** "The historical development between a landscape of habit and a social landscape cannot be put down to a single factor such as enhanced memory, language, discursive consciousness or technology. Neither was it a gradual trend … In Palaeolithic Europe it was both a process of long-term innovation and contingent upon the replacement of population."
- **Notes:** Bridge sentence into empirical chapters.

### Claim R2-047: Ch.4 open — flexible boundaries of Palaeolithic Europe; eastern cul-de-sac problem
- **Register:** FACT / POSITION
- **Target pages:** [[palaeolithic]], [[gamble-clive]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) opens Ch.4 (“The first European societies 500,000–300,000 years ago”) arguing Palaeolithic Europe’s geographical boundaries were flexible: northern ice sheets, western drowned/exposed shelf, southern Gibraltar likely always open (central Mediterranean islands as African stepping-stones at low sea level), weakest eastern boundaries (Ukraine/Russia steppes, Caspian/Black Sea, Turkey/Caucasus/Near East) — emphasizing Europe’s peninsular/cul-de-sac character (Breuil 1912); defining Europe matters for evolutionary scale of selective pressures, not modern political boundaries; nine-region model (Figure 3.1) may suffice for central/western behavioural variation but may be inappropriate for long-term hominid-evolution processes such as first colonization.
- **Quote:** "The geographical boundaries of Palaeolithic Europe were flexible. … But the east provides the weakest set of geographical boundaries for Pleistocene Europe. … the Abbé Breuil once described as a cul-de-sac … The regional model based on latitude, longitude and relief (Figure 3.1) is sufficient for examining variation in Palaeolithic behaviour within central and western Europe (Gamble 1986a), but it may be inappropriate for unravelling the long-term processes involved in hominid evolution."
- **Notes:** Epigraph: Breuil 1912 French quote on European cul-de-sac.

### Claim R2-048: Pleistocene start at 2.3 Myr BP; cooling trend and orbital/tectonic drivers
- **Register:** FACT
- **Target pages:** [[palaeolithic]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) states the first large-scale glaciation / commencement of the Pleistocene is now dated to 2.3 Myr BP (Zagwijn 1992), adding ~700,000 years to the traditional Pleistocene length (Pliocene boundary 1.6–1.8 Myr BP, Aguirre and Pasini 1985); long-term cooling trend from orbital parameters and mountain building (esp. Himalayas/Tibetan plateau; Ruddiman and Raymo 1988), producing recurrent European interglacial (forest/woodland) vs glacial (open vegetation, low sea levels, ice advances over Scandinavia/Britain and southern mountain ice caps).
- **Quote:** "The first large-scale glaciation, and hence the commencement of the Pleistocene period, is now dated to 2.3 Myr BP (Zagwijn 1992). This was a period of strong cooling … Such a timescale adds an additional 700,000 years to the traditional length of the Pleistocene, where the boundary with the Pliocene is fixed at 1.6-1.8 Myr BP"
- **Notes:** Chronology section; many varied conditions within glacial/interglacial categories flagged for later chapters.

### Claim R2-049: Increased climatic cycle amplitude after ~1.5 Myr BP; Bavel interglacial
- **Register:** FACT
- **Target pages:** [[palaeolithic]], [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Claim:** Gamble (1999) reports early cycles after 2.3 Myr BP have weak deep-sea-core signals; Zagwijn (1992) estimates Netherlands July temperatures ~10–20°C average fluctuation 2.3–1.5 Myr BP, then range increases to ~5–20°C with major vegetation repercussions; Netherlands Bavel interglacial at 1 Myr BP is first pollen profile with full interglacial stratigraphic succession (sentence cut by figure OCR at slice end).
- **Quote:** "Zagwijn (1992:figure 2) has estimated, on the basis of the Netherlands pollen record, that July temperatures fluctuated between an average of 10°C to 20°C during the period 2.3-1.5 Myr BP. After this time the range increases, with averages now fluctuating between 5°C and 20°C … One result is seen in the Netherlands Bavel interglacial at 1 Myr BP. This is the first time that a pollen profile shows any stratigraphic succession"
- **Notes:** Slice ends mid-argument in climatic amplitude section; subsequent map figure unreadable (OCR garbage lines 1551–1580) — no further claims.

### Claim R2-050: Entity/OCR flags for main-thread integration
- **Register:** FACT
- **Target pages:** [[gamble-palaeolithic-societies-europe-1999]]
- **Claim:** Range-2 slice integrity notes for integration: (1) starts mid-sentence continuing discussion of 1986 nine-region model — full setup is in range 1; (2) Figure 3.2 tacking diagram OCR largely unusable; (3) Table 3.2 five-person seating row OCR garbled; (4) end of Ch.4 opening figure (likely site distribution map with names e.g. Bilzingsleben reverse-OCR fragments) unreadable — do not treat garbled tokens as site inventory; (5) no binford-bordes debate content in this range; (6) Boxgrove, Schöningen, La Cotte, Maastricht-Belvédère, Kostenki, Atapuerca not substantively treated here (Bilzingsleben and Dolní Věstonice named as type examples only).
- **Quote:** "(slice structural note — no primary quote)"
- **Notes:** Coverage COMPLETE for assigned line file; PARTIAL relative to full Ch.4.
